coredata.dc:title: Improved performance and antifouling properties of thin-film composite polyamide membranes modified with nano-sized bactericidal graphene quantum dots for forward osmosis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Seyedpour, S. Fatemeh
Rahimpour, Ahmad
Shamsabadi, Ahmad Arabi
Soroush, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: Forward osmosis (FO) has applications in desalination, water purification, and wastewater treatment. A major problem in FO is membrane fouling (especially biofouling), which affects adversely the flux efficiency, operating costs, and membrane lifespan. Here, this work addresses this problem via incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the active polyamide layer of thin film composite (TFC) membranes. The incorporation of GQDs leads to the development of thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes that have improved surface hydrophilicity, antimicrobial activity, and FO performance. The functionalization of the polyamide layer by GQDs is verified by XPS and ATR-FTIR. The properties and FO performance evaluation of the TFN membranes are compared with those of the TFC membranes, in terms of membrane morphology, structural properties, permeability, separation ability, and antifouling properties. The TFN membranes exhibited considerable antibacterial activity (about 90–95% reduction in the proliferation of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) as attached live bacteria). This significant antimicrobial activity is due to the unique structure of GQD-functionalized polyamide selective layer (i.e., its higher number of active edges representing oxidation stress). The TFN membranes also exhibited improved water flux in a range of GQD loading, and satisfactory long term stability due to strong covalent interactions between the GQDs and the polyamide active layer. The higher antibacterial activity, better antifouling properties, and the same transport properties of the TFN membranes point to the great FO potential of this new generation of membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Forward osmosis
Thin film nanocomposite
Antibacterial activity
Biofouling mitigation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of TFC and TFN membranes
Effects of the GQD incorporation on the TFN membrane characteristics
Effects of GQDs concentration on membrane transport parameters
Antimicrobial activity of the TFN membranes

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) DLS size distribution, and (c) FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs.

Interfacial polymerization between TMC and MPD monomers, the suggested structure of the prepared TFN membranes, our proposed interaction mechanism of GQDs and polyamide chains, and chain entanglement, where m and n represent the crosslinked and the linear fractions of the polyamide structure, respectively (m
+
n
= 1).
(a) AFM roughness parameters and (b) water contact angle of the TFC and TFN membranes loaded by different concentrations of GQDs, and (c) The ART-FTIR spectra of TFC and TFN membranes.

The SEM micrographs of the TFC and TFN membranes. The surface and cross section images of (a) TFC, (b) TFN-0.05, (c) TFN-0.1, and (d) TFN-0.5. Magnifications of 5 and 1μm for top surface and cross section, respectively.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C (1s) and (b) O (1s) of the TFC and TFN-0.05 membranes.
FO Performance assessment of the TFC and TFN membranes versus the concentration of DS: (A) water flux, (B) reverse solute flux, and (C) Js/Jw ratio.
CFU of TFC and TFN-0.5 membranes obtained by the standard plate count method. (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus cells colonies.

Bacterial inactivation on the membrane surfaces; (a) and (b) SEM micrographs, (c) and (d) fluorescent images of E. coli cells on the membrane surface, stained with SYTO 9 (green), and PI (red) for viable and dead cells, respectively; (a) and (c) TFC, and (b) and (d) TFN membranes.
Long-term antifouling performance of the TFC and TFN-0.1 membranes versus time using a 1M NaCl as draw solution: (a) organic fouling by Alginate and (b) microbial fouling by E. coli.
The atomic compositions, the O/N ratio, and the degree of cross-linking of the polyamide active layer.
Transport parameters of the TFC and TFN membranes (adopting the protocol described elsewhere (Tiraferri et al., 2013)).

head.ce:title: Improved performance and antifouling properties of thin-film composite polyamide membranes modified with nano-sized bactericidal graphene quantum dots for forward osmosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Babol Noushirvani University of Technology, Shariati Ave., Babol, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Drexel University, Philadelphia, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Forward osmosis (FO) has applications in desalination, water purification, and wastewater treatment. A major problem in FO is membrane fouling (especially biofouling), which affects adversely the flux efficiency, operating costs, and membrane lifespan. Here, this work addresses this problem via incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the active polyamide layer of thin film composite (TFC) membranes. The incorporation of GQDs leads to the development of thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes that have improved surface hydrophilicity, antimicrobial activity, and FO performance. The functionalization of the polyamide layer by GQDs is verified by XPS and ATR-FTIR. The properties and FO performance evaluation of the TFN membranes are compared with those of the TFC membranes, in terms of membrane morphology, structural properties, permeability, separation ability, and antifouling properties. The TFN membranes exhibited considerable antibacterial activity (about 90–95% reduction in the proliferation of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) as attached live bacteria). This significant antimicrobial activity is due to the unique structure of GQD-functionalized polyamide selective layer (i.e., its higher number of active edges representing oxidation stress). The TFN membranes also exhibited improved water flux in a range of GQD loading, and satisfactory long term stability due to strong covalent interactions between the GQDs and the polyamide active layer. The higher antibacterial activity, better antifouling properties, and the same transport properties of the TFN membranes point to the great FO potential of this new generation of membranes.

ce:section.ce:para: The climate change, the global depletion of fresh water sources, and the increase in demand for fresh water – because of rapid economic development and population increase – are growing major problems that humanity currently faces. Hence, desalination of brackish and seawater, and wastewater reclamation are two major promising solutions to the fresh water scarcity problem (Mutamim et al., 2013; Shamsuddin et al., 2015; Zhaoliang et al., 2013). The membrane separation technology is an environmentally friendly and efficient way for clean water production and wastewater recovery (Zhao et al., 2014). Among current membrane-based water purification methods, forward osmosis (FO) has developed as an effective new technology that attracted considerable attention in both scientific and industrial communities in the past decade (Chung et al., 2012b; Klaysom et al., 2013; Lutchmiah et al., 2014). Due to its appealing features such as simplicity, high water recovery, low energy consumption, low hydraulic pressure, and acceptable resistance against a wide variety of contaminants (Cath et al., 2006; Chung et al., 2012b; Zhao et al., 2012), FO has great potential to be used in many applications such as seawater desalination (Ang et al., 2006; Dabaghian and Rahimpour, 2015; Zirehpour et al., 2016), waste water treatment (Azari et al., 2014), power generation (Chung et al., 2012a), and food processing (Cath et al., 2006). Nevertheless, membrane fouling (especially biofouling) is a major drawback of FO, as it affects adversely the flux efficiency, operating costs, and membrane lifespan. Furthermore, the lack of an ideal FO membrane with high water permeability, minimal reverse solute flux, and low fouling propensity, and high chemical and mechanical stability has hindered the industrial use of this technology (Ge et al., 2013; Tiraferri et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2012a).

Biological fouling or biofouling is a major issue in membranes processes. It involves the adhesion of microorganisms to the surface of membranes and their growth into microbial communities surrounded in extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) (Flemming et al., 1997). This biofilm formed under the protection of EPS is highly resistant to physical and chemical treatments (Kwan et al., 2015; Rana and Matsuura, 2010; Xie et al., 2015). In desalination processes, biofilms also intensify concentration polarization effects, leading to biofilm enhanced osmotic pressure, subsequent water flux decrease, and salt rejection decline (Herzberg and Elimelech, 2007). In this regards, numerous studies have been performed on biofouling formation mechanisms and control strategies (Li et al., 2015; Mansouri et al., 2010; Matin et al., 2011; Zirehpour et al., 2017a). Anti-biofouling propensity of membranes is often promoted by the incorporation of antimicrobial nanomaterials to the membrane formulation (Castrillón et al., 2014; Li et al., 2015; Pan et al., 2016; Tiraferri et al., 2012; Widjojo et al., 2011; Zirehpour et al., 2017b).

Biocidal nanomaterials such as ZnO (Fu et al., 2015), Cu and Ag nanomaterials (Ben-Sasson et al., 2013; Mauter et al., 2011) have been embedded into the membrane structure, but these nanomaterials have often released out during the water treatment process, leading to secondary contamination (Sun et al., 2014). On the other hand, carbon-based nanomaterials such as one-dimensional carbon nanotubes (CNT) and two-dimensional graphene nanosheets possess remarkable antimicrobial properties due to their cytotoxic mechanisms of oxidative stress and physical piercing (Firouzjaei et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2011b; Perreault et al., 2013; Szunerits and Boukherroub, 2016; Tiraferri et al., 2011).

Among graphene-based materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zero-dimensional graphene sheets with sizes lower than 100nm have appealing characteristics (Li et al., 2015). Generally, GQDs have a single or a few graphene layers with a small size distribution in a range between 3 to 20nm (Ran et al., 2013). When their size is in the range 3–10nm, they have strong quantum confinement and edge effects (Lin et al., 2014; Peng et al., 2012; Ritter and Lyding, 2009). Additionally, GQDs have large surface area and fine surface grafting ascribed to functional surface groups (Lin et al., 2014). Similar to graphene oxide, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups of GQDs at their surfaces exhibit exceptional water solubility and compatibility with various polymeric, organic/inorganic, and biological species (Jafari et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2012; Tetsuka et al., 2012). Also, GQDs have been used in antibacterial research due to their exceptional electron transport property resulting in enhanced peroxidase activity compared to graphene nanosheets (Dong et al., 2012a; Essig et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2011a; Lu et al., 2011). They have also been found to improve bactericidal and antibiofouling properties of polyvinylidene difluoride membranes significantly (Zeng et al., 2016). However, to the best of the authors’ knowledge, GQDs have not been investigated as bactericidal reagents in TFC FO membranes.

In this study, GQDs were incorporated into TFC membranes to investigate their effects on the FO performance, antimicrobial properties, and antibiofouling behavior of the resulting TFN membranes. Besides, the antibacterial properties of the TFN membranes were assessed and compared with those of the TFC membranes, using two model microorganisms, S. aureus and E. coli. The cell viability study was conducted using a simple colony-counting method and fluorescent imaging. Morphological changes in the bacteria were also investigated by SEM after bacterial adhesion tests.

Polyethersulfone (PES) (Ultrason E6020P, Mw =58,000g/mol) as polymer, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (99.5%, scharlau) as solvent, Triton X-100 (Merck) and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) (Mw =25,000g/mol, Merck) as additives and pore formers were used to prepare the casting solution of the membrane substrates. Trimesoylchloride (TMC), 1,3-phenylendiamine (MPD), Triethylamine (TEA), Camphorsulfonic acid (CSA) from Merck and n-hexane (96%, scharlau) were used for the synthesis of polyamide layers. Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%) for the preparation of FO draw solution (DS) and citric acid (CA) for the synthesis of GQDs were purchased from Merck.

GQDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis of CA according to the procedure previously reported elsewhere (Dong et al., 2012b; He et al., 2014). and then GQDs nanoparticles were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FTIR and DLS. The detail of synthesis method is described in supporting information (SI 1.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PES membrane substrates were synthesized by the conventional phase inversion induced by the immersion precipitation technique (Zirehpour et al., 2015). Further information on PES substrate preparation is presented in the SI1.3.1. Thin polyamide active layer of both TFC and TFN FO membranes were prepared via interfacial polymerization process between MPD and TMC on PES substrates. The polyamide TFC membrane was fabricated by immersing the PES substrate in an aqueous solution containing 2wt% MPD, 2wt% CSA and 1wt% TEA for 2min. The amine saturated membrane was then immersed in a 0.1wt/v% TMC in n-hexane solution for 30s Afterwards, the membrane was subjected to an oven at 80°C for 5min to ensure the formation of an ultrathin polyamide selective layer. The TFN membranes were prepared by introducing different amounts of GQDs (0.05, 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5wt%) into an aqueous solution during the IP process; the resulting membranes hereafter are denoted as TFN-0.05, TFN-0.1, TFN-0.3 and TFN-0.5, respectively.

The cross-section and top-surface morphologies of the membranes were evaluated using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, MIRA3 TESCAN). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was also used to study the surface tophology of membranes. Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR, Thermo Scientific, USA) spectra were used to detect the functional groups of the TFN active layer. The surface hydrophilicity of the membranes was evaluated by means of a contact angle measuring instrument (Dataphysics, OCA 15 plus). The elemental composition and chemical binding of the polyamide layers were studied using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Bestec, Germany) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source.

Fouling experiments of the membranes were accomplished uisng a closed-loop lab-scale cross-flow FO unit with an active sample area of 30cm2, and feed and draw volumes of 5L. The selective layer faced the feed solution. To circulate the feed and draw solutions in a closed-loop configuration, two gear pumps were applied. To obtain a flux of 20±1 Lm−2h−1 as the initial flux, 1M NaCl solution was used as the draw solution. A baseline experiment was conducted for each of the TFC and TFN membranes in the absence of foulants (without sodium alginate or bacteria) to account for the draw solution dilution and reverse flux through the FO membranes. After the operation of the FO unit at a flow rate of 8.5cms−1 and a temperature of 25±1°C for 24h, the permeate flux was calculated based on the mass of the feed-solution tank measured using an electric balance (AND EK6000I).

In case of organic fouling, sodium alginate (Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, MO), a polysaccharide, was chosen as a model organic foulant. A solution was prepared by mixing 250mg/L of the sodium alginate powder in deionized water. After flux stabilization, the prepared solution was introduced to the feed solution to accelerate the fouling experiments.

Before each biofouling test, the system was washed and disinfected by deionized water and ethanol solution (70% v) for 1h. Next, the FO system was washed three times with deionized water to remove the cleaning reagent completely. S. aureus and E. coli were cultured in sterile LB medium overnight at 37°C and then diluted to an OD600 of 0.25±0.4 (Ben-Sasson et al., 2013; Perreault et al., 2013). After flux stabilization, the feed solution (sterile isotonic solution with ionic strength of 15mM) was inoculated with 50mL of each bacterial culture to achieve an initial concentration of ∼107 CFU L−1. Physical cleaning tests were carried out instantaneously after the FO membrane fouling runs, using a solution without foulant (deionized water) circulated in both feed and draw sides for 45min at a cross-flow velocity of 25cm/s. Next, the water flux of the cleaned membrane was recalculated using the same experimental procedure explained for the baseline test. Further information on the procedural details of antibacterial activity assessment and bacterial viability, fluorescent scanning microscopy, FO membrane performance, and characterization are presented in the SI.

To confirm the formation of the GQDs, TEM (Fig. 1 a), DLS (Fig. 1b) and FTIR (Fig. 1c) analyses were conducted and the GQDs were characterized. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the TEM image of the as-prepared GQDs exhibits uniform and an approximately-spherical morphology. The GQDs mostly displayed an average size distribution of 3.4–8.8nm, as presented in Fig. 1b. To confirm the formation of GQDs, its surface chemistry was further studied using FTIR and the detail of characterizations is presented in SI2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the GQD incorporation on the TFN membrane characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows a schematic of the interfacial polymerization between MPD and TMC. During the first few hours of the experiment at ambient temperature, the MPD solution containing GQDs undergoes a color change from transparent to dark green and finally black, possibly due to oxidation (Ali et al., 2016). When GQDs is incorporated into the MPD aqueous solution, they can interact with MPD and TMC monomers during the polymerization (Shen et al., 2016), allowing for the incorporation of GQDs into the PA structure at this stage (as shown in Fig. 2) (Bano et al., 2015; Saha and Joshi, 2009). In addition to the hydrogen bonds, the amine functional groups in the MPD monomer may react with GQDs and create new amide bonds during ultrasonication of MPD solution (Shen et al., 2016). Besides, the creation of anhydride and ester linkages is anticipated due to interactions between acyl chloride groups of TMC and functional groups of GQDs (Shen et al., 2016). The unreacted TMC acyl chloride groups may possibly interact with the carboxyl groups of GQDs during interfacial polymerization. In other words, hydrogen bonds can be formed with interactions between GQDs functional groups and primary and secondary amines. Moreover, covalent bonds between GQDs and the carboxyl groups in the linear fraction of the polyamide may be produced through condensation reactions (Ali et al., 2016).

The ATR-FTIR spectra of TFC and TFN membranes represented in Fig. 3 c verify the formation of the polyamide layer. The peak at 850cm−1 corresponds to the C–Cl stretching vibration of the unreacted acyl chloride group. The peaks at 1152 and 1312cm−1 contributed to the symmetric and asymmetric O=SO stretching vibrations of the polyethersulfone support layer, respectively. Two peaks observed at around 1407 and 1480cm−1 correspond to C–C in aromatic ring (Ghanbari et al., 2015; Rahimpour et al., 2012). The peak detected at around 1660cm−1 resulted from the amide CO stretching vibrations, whereas the band appeared around 1575cm−1 is mainly attributed to interactions of C–N stretching and in-plane N–H amide II bending vibrations (Ali et al., 2016; Bano et al., 2015). After incorporation of the GQD into the PA matrix, the intensity of most of peaks decreased consideably and some of them disapeared completely by increasing the content of GQD, (Ghorbani et al., 2016) representative of the interaction of GQD with PA matrix (Rahimpour et al., 2018). Interstingly, the considerable decrement of the of acyl chloride intensiy (550–730cm−1) (Silverstein et al., 2014) by inceasing the content of GQD in the PA layer indicates new bond formation between GQD and unreacted acyl chloride of PA chain. Additionally, the increased stretching vibration of CO bond in GQD embedded membrane demonstrates the formation of the new amide linkages by the interaction of carboxyl group of GQDs and amine group of MPD (Bano et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2016). Meanwhile, the peak occurring at around 1730cm−1 of the TFN membranes ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO in the ester groups created through interactions between the functional groups of GQDs and the carboxylic groups of the polyamide selective layer (Ali et al., 2016). Compared to the TFC membrane, the TFN membranes also revealed higher intensity and broader peak around 3300cm−1 that can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of –OH functional groups of GQDs,leading to improved surface hydrophilicity (Wang et al., 2012b; Zhao et al., 2013).

The mean roughness (Ra), root-mean-squared roughness (Rrms), and the mean difference between the highest peaks and lowest valleys (Rz ) of the TFC and TFN membranes are presented in Fig. 3a. The results reveal that the TFN membranes appear to be slightly smoother than the TFC membranes. Moreover, the roughness characteristics of the TFN membranes decreased slightly by increasing the concentration of GQDs, indicating a proper dispersion of GQDs in the selective layer. A good dispersion of GQDs not only can smoothen the membrane surface (Qiu et al., 2009) but also can contribute to efficient formation of the polyamide layer through interfacial polymerization (Kim et al., 2015). Compared to the TFC membranes with rough surface, the TFN membranes exhibited denser and smoother surfaces. According to the previous studies (Shen et al., 2016), the smoother TFN membranes can be due to three reasons. First, the diffusion of MPD generally creates a ridge and valley morphology, whereas the horizontally oriented GQDs hinder the MPD penetration to the organic phase, which leads to the formation of a smoother surface with a leaf-like structure. Second, the functional groups on the surface of GQDs interact with MPD and TMC monomers during the interfacial polymerization, and the interactions affect the rate of reaction of MPD and TMC (Shen et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2015). Third, the hydrogen bonds in the functional groups of GQDs can allow the formation of a denser polymer structure (Bano et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2016). Fig. 2 shows a schematic of our proposed interaction mechanism of GQDs and the TFN membranes.

Fig. 3b shows the contact angle values of the TFN and pristine TFC membranes. The incorporation of GQDs significantly improved the selective layer hydrophilicity, decreasing the water contact angle from 72.9° (for TFC) to 51° (for TFN-0.5). As discussed earlier, the significant enhancement in the hydrophilicity of the TFN membrane can be ascribed to the existence of functional groups with abundant oxygen containing groups on the GQD surface (Bano et al., 2015). The surface hydrophilicity increase is a good strategy to decrease foulant adhesion and thereby improve antifouling propensity (Kochkodan and Hilal, 2015; Rana and Matsuura, 2010; Tiraferri et al., 2012).

Fig. 4 depicts the cross section and top-surface SEM micrographs of the TFC and TFN membranes. The higher-magnification FE-SEM image indicates that the overall surface morphology TFN membranes are affected upon the addition of GQDs.

The alteration in the structure of the membrane can be ascribed to the acceleration of interfacial polymerization triggered by incorporation of hydrophilic GQDs (Baroña et al., 2013; Lind et al., 2009). Furthermore, the cross-sectional morphology (Fig. 4) shows that by loading of GQDs, the average thickness of the PA selective layer decreases substantially at first and then enhances as the concentration increases (Zhao et al., 2015).

The steric hindrance of the GQDs diminishes the MPD penetration and retards the formation of the PA layer (Song et al., 2016). The mass transfer resistance decreases considerably with this decrease in the thickness of active layer and therefore leads to an improvement in the water flux (Xia et al., 2015). However, by using a higher concentration of GQDs, the overall thickness of polyamide selective layer of the TFN membranes increased and a denser layer formed compared to that of the pristine TFC membranes owing to higher viscosity of the MPD solution, which leads to surface blockage and severe reduction in the membrane permeability (Mural et al., 2014). Nonetheless, it should be mentioned that the higher loading rates results in the GQDs leakage from the PA layer, more agglomerations, and a thicker active layer (Ali et al., 2016; Song et al., 2016).

Fig. 5 illustrates the XPS surveys spectra of TFC and TFN membranes, indicating the existence of three predominantly components, namely carbon (C 1s), oxygen (O 1s), and nitrogen (N 1s). The relative elemental compositions are summarized in Table 1 ; they were determined based on the intensity of the C(1s), N(1s), O(1s) peaks located at around 285, 399, and 532eV, respectively (Khorshidi et al., 2015).

The atomic compositions results revealed that the embedment of GQDs into the MPD solution leads to a higher O/N ratio for TFN membrane. Despite the fact that the increased O/N ratio can be indicative of a polyamide layer with lower degree of cross-linking, this increment can be corresponded to the numerous oxygen containing functional groups on the GQDs surface. Some of the GQDs most presumably interact with the amine terminal of the MPD or acyl chloride functional group of TMC monomers and finally are introduced to the polyamide structure during the process IP. Accordingly, the increased O/N ratio can be attributed to both decrease of cross-linking degree or incorporation of GQDs to the PA chain. On account of organic nature of GQDs, there is proper compatibility between GQDs to the PA chain. However, the incorporation of a high concentration of GQDs interferes with the polymerization process, weakening the intermolecular linkages between the monomer functional groups and causing weak PA layer integrity (Lai et al., 2016).

Additional information on the chemical bonding was obtained from the high resolution XPS spectra of O (1s) and C (1s) shown in Figs. 8 a and 8b. The results of C (1s) high resolution spectra for both TFC and TFN membranes indicate three basic peaks: a main peak at 285eV corresponding to an aromatic/aliphatic carbon in the C–H or C–C bonds without electron withdrawing (Benavente and Vázquez, 2004; Boussu et al., 2007; Tang et al., 2007); a middle peak at 286.5eV corresponding to carbon atoms in an environment with poor electron-withdrawing (carbons in C–N bond); and a weak peak at 288.5eV assigned to carbons connected to groups with strong electron withdrawing (carbons in amides OC–N and carboxylic OC–O bonds) (Tang et al., 2009). The de-convoluted peak under the C (1s) spectrum of the TFN membrane shows that the intensity of C–N bond diminished considerably after incorporation of GQDs. In the other words, some of the amine functions are consumed as a result of the new amide linkages between –COOH groups of GQDs and –NH2 groups of MPD and consequently are introduced to the polyamide structure during interfacial polymerization, further verified by the FTIR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of GQDs concentration on membrane transport parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of GQDs concentration on the intrinsic transport properties of the TFC and TFN membranes were investigated and the results are summarized in Table 2 . The water permeability (A), the solute permeability (B), and the structural parameter (S) were determined using a four-step procedure (Tiraferri et al., 2013) and at least three independent FO experiments. The water permeability enhanced initially and then decreased with the GQDs concentration. Initially, the water permeability enhanced up to 29% for TFN-0.05 and 49% for TFN-0.1, while the salt permeability of TFN-0.1 was considerably lower than that of the TFC membrane. A higher loading of GQDs significantly decreases the water permeability (in case of TFN-0.5 membrane). Similarly, the incorporation of GQDs diminished the solute permeability/water permeability ratio (B/A) of the TFN membranes, providing an enhanced membrane permselectivity (Table 2). In general, a lower B/A ratio offers a better membrane selectivity and is favourable for FO systems, as it enhances selectivity and reduces fouling tendency (Lind et al., 2009; Tang et al., 2010). The B/A ratio of the TFN-0.1 membrane is slightly smaller than that of the TFN-0.05 one and has the lowest value among all the membranes.

These observations suggest that the incorporation of the GQDs to the thin active layer increases the hydrophilicity of membrane surface by facilitating an accelerated water molecules transport across the membrane (Bano et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2013). As supported by SEM results, it also decreases the selective layer thickness and leads to a lower transport resistance and consequently improves the water flux. Moreover, it may provide extra nano-channels for transport of water molecules by the interfacial gap between GQD nanosheets and the polyamide structure (Hu and Mi, 2013; Huang et al., 2014; Mahmoud et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). Alternatively, it may affect PA chain packing density and result in inevitably increased free volume in the thin selective layer (Song et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2013; Yin et al., 2012), as suggested by the XPS results (Table 1). On the other hand, higher concentrations of GQDs decreased the water flux and caused higher selectivity by thickening the selective layer (Zhao et al., 2015). Nevertheless, excessive incorporation of GQDs did not provide high selectivity due to the aggregation and uneven distribution of GQDs (Bano et al., 2015).

Fig. 6 a,b presents the water flux (Jw) and solute flux (Js) of the TFC and TFN membranes as a function of the DS concentration, respectively. The Jw enhanced by introducing GQDs to the polyamide selective layer, providing additional pass for the transport of water molecule and a more hydrophilic surface compared to that of a pristine TFC membrane (Fig. 6a). Conversely, Js decreased by increasing the GQDs content and the TFC-0.5 showed the lowest value among all membranes (Fig. 6b). Totally, the specific solute flux (Js/Jw) is an important parameter in a FO process, representing membrane performance in terms of selectivity and productivity. The same as the B/A ratio, a lower Js/Jw value is favorable for both higher productivity and selectivity. Fig. 6c depicts the Js/Jw value of the TFC and TFN membranes. A considerable decrease in Js/Jw of the TFC-0.1 membrane indicates significant enhancement in both productivity and selectivity at this level of GQDs incorporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of the TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the antimicrobial activities of membrane surfaces against S. aureus and E. coli demonstrated that even during a short contact period (1h incubation), the number of attached viable bacteria remarkably decreased on the surface of the TFN membrane. In comparison to the pristine TFC membrane, TFN-0.5 exhibited over 90 and 95% bacterial inactivation (normalized number of live bacteria) for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively (Fig. 7 ). As reported previously (Hui et al., 2015; Ristic et al., 2014), GQDs also exhibited a strong antibacterial activity against S. aureus; that is indicating the high ability of GQDs to interrupt the integrity of bacterial cell membrane. This superior antimicrobial activity of the TFN membranes compared to that of the pristine TFC membranes can be ascribed to the low aggregation and uniform dispersion of GQDs with a higher portion of active edges on the surface of membrane, enhancing the contact to bacterial cells and thus surface cytotoxicity. In addition, the TFN membranes were examined after one week immersion in water to evaluate long-term stability of the antibacterial activity. The results revealed that, despite slight increase in proliferation activities, there was no considerable change in the bactericidal activity, validating the key advantage of GQDs antibacterial activity even after the embedment of GQDs into the active layers of the TFN membranes.

To further study the antibacterial properties of the TFN membranes, the morphological defects in the bacteria cell membranes were visualized by SEM. As can be seen in Fig. 8 a, there is a clear difference between the cell morphologies of the live and dead bacteria. Most of E. coli cells in contact with the surface of a pristine TFC membrane maintain their original structure with no physical disruption in cell membranes, whereas the cells attached to a TFN membrane lost their membrane integrity (had a flattened or shrunken structure). Furthermore, the antibacterial activity of the TFC and TFN membranes were observed by fluorescence images after contacting with E. coli bacterial suspension (Fig. 8b). The number of live bacteria (green spots) on the TFC membrane surfaces is much higher than on the TFN membranes, suggesting enhanced antibacterial activities, which is in agreement with the bacteria cell viability results. The strong antibacterial properties of membranes may be caused by the presence of active GQDs nanosheets, which impart antimicrobial properties to the membrane surface, as the E. coli cell integrity disruption confirms.

Despite the fact that most carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene and fullerenes as well as GO display antibacterial activities (Kang et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2011b; Lyon et al., 2006), the mechanism of GQDs cytotoxicity on the bacteria has not been understood completely yet. Several mechanisms have been suggested for the antibacterial activity of graphene oxide (GO), comprising membrane stress, oxidative stress, entrapment, and the basal plane (Shi et al., 2016). The antibacterial activity of GO against E. coli has been contributed to membrane and oxidative stress, both associated with aggregation state and surface functional groups (Liu et al., 2011b). Although a membrane decorated with GO nanosheets can possess antibacterial property against E. coli, the nanosheets cannot completely cover the surface of the membrane with high distribution due to their relatively swollen lateral size and wider size distribution (Zeng et al., 2016). Furthermore, past studies suggested that highly-dispersive ultra-small GQDs with excellent specific surface areas can accomplish a contact-inhibition mode. Because of the strong drag forces originated from the graphene structure, this mode is advantageous for direct insertion/cutting of lipid membranes and severe extraction of phospholipid molecules, leading to severe damages to the cell membrane and hence the release of cytoplasm (Zeng et al., 2016).

As shown in Fig. 9 a,b, once the foulants were added to the feed solution, the fluxes decreased dramatically and then reached steady state after 21h of fouling test. The results indicate that the antifouling capacity of the TFN membrane significantly improved by incorporating GQDs. In addition, water flux recoveries of 65% and 35% were achieved, respectively, for the TFC and TFN membranes after a 45min cleaning. Generally, the improved antifouling properties can be due to the hydrophilicity enhancement and more negatively charged surface of the TFN membrane containing appropriate content of GQDs (Goosen et al., 2005; Song et al., 2016). The former can diminish the accumulation of foulants on the surface of membrane, resulting in a better flux recovery, which suggests the lower fouling propensity (Hwang et al., 2013), while the latter can generate stronger electrostatic repulsion between the foulants and the surface of the TFN membrane (Ali et al., 2016; Bernstein et al., 2011).

Due to the development of a biofilm on the surface of the membrane, a gradual reduction was observed in the permeate flux over the course of 24h. The TFC and TFN membranes had flux declines of 40% and 20%, respectively. This result suggests that the TFN membrane possesses higher anti-biofouling propensity in comparison to the pristine TFC membrane. These findings are consistent with the previous results that a GQD-functionalized membrane is more effective than a GO-functionalized membrane in preventing the multiplication of bacteria and the formation of biofilm (Zeng et al., 2016).

This work highlights that the new GQD-functionalized TFC membranes (TFN membranes) possess outstanding FO performance in terms of permeability, selectivity, and antifouling activities. The potential of GQDs directly comes from their graphitic structure, quantum nature, and colloidal stability in a variety of solvents. Due to simultaneous increases in the membrane surface hydrophilicity and the antibacterial activity, the incorporation of GQDs results in a remarkable improvement in the antifouling property without sacrificing the TFN membrane performance. These attractive features are due to favorable changes in membrane hydrophilicity, stability, antibiofouling, and antibacterial properties, caused by the incorporation of GQDs to the polyamide selective layer. These results indicate that GQDs are promising nanomaterials for the development of relatively low cost polyamide membranes for use in desalination and wastewater treatment.
